radicals.meaningMnemonic: This radical is pretty complicated, but not much looks like this radical, so you're in luck. This looks like a giant flying airship of some kind. See the propellers on the back as it moves to the left? Just imagine a kind of steampunk airship like this flying through the air. That's why this radical is <radical>fly</radical> — because it's flying.

That's why this radical is <radical>fly</radical> — because it's flying.

radicals.meaningMnemonic: This radical looks like a combination of the brush radical (聿) and the beauty kanji (美). So what's something beautiful painted with a brush? The most famous example is probably the <radical>Mona Lisa</radical>. This Mona Lisa just happens to have some horns sketched on… by you. Oh, hey, you've just vandalized the Mona Lisa. Nice one.

radicals.meaningMnemonic: The three lines that make up the river radical are being warped to the left here. Probably by a massive force of rushing water, because this is a <radical>flood</radical>. RUN!

radicals.meaningMnemonic:
This radical looks like a <radical>mole</radical>. Picture its head poking out of a pile of dirt and looking up to the sky, whiskers twitching.

Alternately, here's a mnemonic: What lives under the ground in private places covered in dirt? A mole! Sure, its walls, ceiling, and floors are all dirt, but that's what home is to a mole.

radicals.meaningMnemonic: The horns on the king give him an ego. Which in turn gives him a sense of <reading>righteousness</reading>. He shouldn't act all righteous like that, but I suppose it's what kings do. If you had horns and a big ego, you'd probably think you were the epitome of righteousness too.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: If you put your <radical>mouth</radical> on a <radical>tree</radical> and try to eat it, you’re probably a <kanji>distressed</kanji> and <kanji>troubled</kanji> individual (unless you’re a woodchuck). You don’t need to eat this tree, you have plenty of food at home. Get your mouth off that tree you distressed child.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine yourself as this distressed and troubled person. Taste the taste of sap and bark in your mouth as you nom on the tree with all your might.
kanji.readingMnemonic: You thought you were the most <kanji>distressed</kanji> and <kanji>troubled</kanji> person in this situation, but then you look up and see a very equally distressed and troubled <reading>con</reading>dor (こん). The tree you've been mouthing is its home. And now you've slobbered all over it, ruining this condor's day.
kanji.readingHint: Picture a very distressed condor watching you munch your mouth all over its tree house. You've made a condor enemy this day.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: A <radical>flood</radical> and a <radical>fire</radical>! At the same time? This is a <kanji>disaster</kanji>!
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine a disaster so bad that there are floods AND fires. That's too much!
kanji.readingMnemonic: This <kanji>disaster</kanji> was caused by your <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい)! It's malfunctioning, running around leaking large amounts of water and breathing fire. I guess you didn't work out all the kinks before you let it roam wild.
kanji.readingHint: You better figure out a way to stop your cyborg from causing more disasters like this. Fix it now, or you're gonna be in big trouble.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Next to a <radical>tree</radical>, you spot <radical>poop</radical>, not once but twice. Only a <radical>drunkard</radical> would be the type of <radical>person</radical> to not use the nearby <kanji>machine</kanji> toilet. There's a perfectly good outhouse with a machine toilet right there, but this drunkard is so drunk that he decides to poop under the tree instead. And not only does he poop once, he poops twice.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine this drunkard, squatting down under the tree to poop. You can smell the poop and a faint stench of alcohol in the air. How could he not have realized that there was a machine toilet right there?
kanji.readingMnemonic: Oh, I see — the door to the outhouse with the <kanji>machine</kanji> toilet is locked, and the drunkard doesn't have the <reading>key</reading> (き). If he had a machine like a jackhammer, or a circular saw, or some other heavy duty machine, he might be able to get in without a key, but since he has none of those or a key, he's reduced to pooping under the tree instead.
kanji.readingHint: Listen to the drunkard grumbling about the lack of a key as he poops under the tree. He doesn't sound happy, and every time he stresses to push out the poop, his voice fluctuates. Imagine hearing this in your mind and being a wee bit disgusted.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Sitting on the <radical>lid</radical> of a toilet, you're praying your <radical>poop</radical> <radical>explosion</radical> will be flushed away, beseeching the <radical>cross</radical> decorating the bathroom wall. The poop needs to be one hundred <kanji>percent</kanji> flushed away because you're on a date, and you don't want even one percent of your explosion left for them to see.
kanji.meaningHint: You take a peek under the lid to check, and your poop explosion is one hundred percent gone! Phew!
kanji.readingMnemonic: It was extra important that there was zero <kanji>percent</kanji> poop left, because you're not on a date just anywhere, you're at the <reading>Ritz</reading> (りつ)!
kanji.readingHint: Look around you at the classy surroundings of the Ritz Hotel bathroom. You feel one hundred percent relieved now, and ready to enjoy your date at the Ritz!

kanji.meaningMnemonic: The <radical>fly</radical> radical and the <kanji>fly</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Awesome!
kanji.meaningHint: Thanks to that radical knowledge, you're flying through these kanji now.
kanji.readingMnemonic: When you <kanji>fly</kanji>, you generate a lot of <reading>he</reading>at (ひ) because you're moving so fast through the atmosphere. Normally this heat would be a nuisance, but you're a superhero so it doesn't bother you. This is actually the reason you're able to fly — you're a superhero and super resistant to heat.
kanji.readingHint: You're flying through the air super fast. Feel the air rushing past you as heat builds up all over your body from rocketing through the air so fast.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You slip off the <radical>roof</radical> and bang up your <radical>rib cage</radical> and <radical>mouth</radical>. Oof, that's some serious <kanji>damage</kanji>. How will you explain this <kanji>injury</kanji> to your pals at the We Never Fall Off of Roofs Club?
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine the moment you fall off the roof. In slow motion you can see the roof moving away, then BAM! You've injured your rib cage and mouth. Ow! If only you were a video game character that never takes fall damage.
kanji.readingMnemonic: As you try to assess the <kanji>damage</kanji>, you hear someone shout "<reading>Guy</reading> (がい)! Hey, you okay, guy?" They run up to you, but your rib cage and mouth are too damaged for you to respond. "Okay guy, just hang in there, guy. I'm gonna get you to the hospital and we'll treat that <kanji>injury</kanji>, guy."
kanji.readingHint: "Don't worry, my guy," your rescuer says. "I'm gonna call up those paramedic guys, and they're gonna get you in to see one of those doctor guys right away. You just hang in there, big guy!"

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Regardless of whether or not you wear a <radical>hat</radical> while you <radical>jackhammer</radical>, you’re going to have a <kanji>surplus</kanji> of noise. Jackhammers are loud, and your hat is doing nothing to dampen the racket. There’s a surplus of commotion coming from the jackhammer and onlookers are not amused.
kanji.meaningHint: The hat you’re wearing is ridiculous, giving you a surplus of bad style. Imagine a bunch of people scowling at you, not only because of your crazy hat, but because of the annoying sound of the jackhammer. Hear the obnoxious noise around you as the jackhammer rattles your bones.
kanji.readingMnemonic: One of the people walking by is the owner of a push cart <reading>yo</reading>-yo (よ) stand. Having a <kanji>surplus</kanji> of yo-yos, he starts throwing them at you. This makes you stop using the jackhammer as you need both hands to fend off the yo-yo barrage.
kanji.readingHint: Your hat is doing little to defend you from the onslaught of yo-yos being tossed at you. Feel the yo-yos hitting your body and listen to them roll away after striking you.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: A <radical>Chinese</radical> <radical>turkey</radical> is <kanji>difficult</kanji> to find because they are so rare. Chinese turkeys are the rarest variety of turkey, so naturally, finding one is exceedingly difficult.
kanji.meaningHint: Try and picture a Chinese turkey in your brain. Now imagine yourself trying to catch it, but you just can’t. It’s too difficult. The Chinese turkey is too elusive.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Do you know the actual reason Chinese turkey are so <kanji>difficult</kanji> to find? It’s because they’re all made out of <reading>Naan</reading> (なん). Of course they’re elusive when they blend in so well with all the other Naan!
kanji.readingHint: Picture yourself entering a bakery, frantically scouring through all the Naan in an attempt to find one that’s actually a Chinese turkey. "WHERE ARE THE TURKEYS?", you scream, to everyone else’s bewilderment. Those elusive, difficult to find Chinese Naan turkeys…

kanji.meaningMnemonic: That <radical>outfit</radical> on your <radical>skin</radical> is there to <kanji>cover</kanji> you up. In polite society, it's expected that you cover your skin with some kind of outfit — even if you have to wear something that causes you to <kanji>incur</kanji> damage, like circulation-cutting skinny jeans or a shirt with a really scratchy tag.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine the most uncomfortable outfit you can. Your body is incurring some minor damage right now, but hey — at least you're covering your skin properly.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Another reason to <kanji>cover</kanji> yourself is to retain <reading>he</reading>at (ひ). Actually, retaining heat and staying warm is probably the reason people started covering themselves up in the first place.
kanji.readingHint: Even if you incur some damage from uncomfortable clothing, it's better to cover up and retain heat than to underdress and incur damage from frostbite or something.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You have an <radical>outfit</radical> <radical>valley</radical>, which is a valley where you store your <kanji>abundant</kanji> number of outfits. Yes, you're so <kanji>wealthy</kanji> that not only can you purchase a valley for yourself, you can also afford to use it like a walk-in closet.
kanji.meaningHint: See the green mountains rising on either side of the valley, hear the birdsong in the distance… and behold the miles of clothes racks that hold all your outfits. This valley is the only space big enough to contain your material abundance and wealth.
kanji.readingMnemonic: This valley is also <kanji>abundant</kanji> with <kanji>wealthy</kanji> <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう) — trendy, wealthy youths who come from around the world to descend on your valley and peep your outfit collection.
kanji.readingHint: Picture these wealthy youths as they peruse your valley of outfits. They're mostly influencers making short-form video content for their socials, basically leeching off your abundance. Ugh. Is this how people get wealthy in the 21st century?

kanji.meaningMnemonic: <radical>Rain</radical> causes a <radical>landslide</radical> that causes an <kanji>earthquake</kanji>!!! The earth doesn't just slide, it <kanji>shake</kanji>s and <kanji>quake</kanji>s.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture this happening in real time. First it starts to rain really hard, then the ground starts to shift and run, then everything is shaking and quaking so violently it's an earthquake!
kanji.readingMnemonic: The <kanji>earthquake</kanji> shakes you so violently that you fall over, banging your <reading>shin</reading> (しん) on the coffee table. The edge of the coffee table is very sharp, so it cuts into your shin pretty badly.
kanji.readingHint: You grab your shin, but the earthquake shakes the coffee table toward you to hit your shin yet again! Ow ow ow stop!

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You take some <radical>flag</radical>s, <radical>nine</radical> in total, and put them one by one up your <kanji>butt</kanji>.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine taking these nine flags... count 'em... and putting them up your butt. With each one that's up there it gets harder and harder to fit the next one in.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Now that you have nine flags inside your <kanji>butt</kanji>, you have to get them out. But, you don't know how to. So you ask <reading>Siri</reading> (しり), "How do I get nine flags out of my butt?"

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You see a <radical>flag</radical> stuck in the ground with a little bit of <radical>fur</radical> coming out from it and on closer inspection you realize it’s actually a <kanji>tail</kanji>. Someone stuck a flag into some sort of animal, pushing it under the ground. All that’s visible now is some fur and part of the tail.
kanji.meaningHint: What kind of animal could it be? You start to feel the soft fur with your hand and then the stench of death hits your nostrils. Ew.
kanji.readingMnemonic: You try to pull the flag out of the ground and hear a rumbling buzzing sound. At the end of the flagpole you see a huge <reading>bee</reading>hive (び)! These bees made their home under the <kanji>tail</kanji>! Maybe that’s why the flag was sticking in it… to warn people. Oops.
kanji.readingHint: See the bees swarming out from around the tail and toward your face. Run! Run before there’s nothing left of you but your own tail!

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Under a <radical>tree</radical> a <radical>drunkard</radical> sits with <radical>twenty</radical> little <kanji>contraptions</kanji>. There's little electronic ones, little bopping ones, little walking ones... twenty of them.
kanji.meaningHint: Can you even imagine twenty completely unique contraptions? Why don't you try, just for fun.
kanji.readingMnemonic: You want to know what these <kanji>contraptions</kanji> are for, so you ask. The drunkard says, "These are <reading>kay</reading>ak (かい) contraptions *urp* they're for your uh *blarp* kayak... things."
kanji.readingHint: "Kayak contraptions" is pretty vague, but you do have a kayak, so you're tempted anyway. If only you could figure out what each contraption did so you could justify buying some.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Under any <radical>cliff</radical>, there's some <radical>dirt</radical> that's under a lot of <kanji>pressure</kanji>.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine being down there under that heavy cliff. Imagine feeling the cliff's pressure as you watch it compress the dirt. It's so hard to breathe... h... help!
kanji.readingMnemonic: You want to increase the <kanji>pressure</kanji>, so you stack on a second cliff. "<reading>Ah, two</reading> (あつ)?!" the dirt cries out in dismay. This dirt is tough, but it's really starting to feel the pressure now.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine yelling out to the dirt to encourage it: "Ah, two cliffs is nothing! Come on, you got this!" You're the dirt's gym buddy, and you won't let it buckle under the pressure.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Someone stabbed the <radical>leader</radical> <radical>mole</radical> with a <radical>knife</radical> to <kanji>overthrow</kanji> it. Now there is a new mole leader. Because that's what happens when you overthrow someone—someone else needs to take over. In this case, a vengeful mole.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine a mole sneaking up behind the leader mole and stabbing him with a knife. This type of overthrowing of leadership feels familiar to you. Maybe this has happened before in the past?
kanji.readingMnemonic: Where do leaders get <kanji>overthrow</kanji>n the most? In <reading>とう</reading>きょう. Just think back in Japanese history, back when everyone was at war with one another. People were taking over and getting overthrown and then that guy gets overthrown and then THAT guy gets overthrown. Basically, it used to be hard to stay a leader in とうきょう.
kanji.readingHint: Picture this like an exaggerated reenactment on the History channel. Leader after leader of とうきょう gets overthrown and replaced with the next guy. It never stops. (Until the end of the Edo period, I guess.)

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Hanging off the edge of a <radical>cliff</radical> is a <radical>sun</radical>-worshipping <radical>child</radical> with a <kanji>thick</kanji> unibrow. Hanging off the cliff, the child cries out to the sun to save them from this cruel fate. Even the thickness of their unibrow is not enough to give the child the strength to climb back up on their own.
kanji.meaningHint: I mean, this unibrow is really, really thick. So thick it doesn’t even look real. But it is. It’s really real and really thick.
kanji.readingMnemonic: The child glances up at their <kanji>thick</kanji> unibrow and yells, "<reading>ah, two</reading> (あつ)!" Yup, it turns out the sun misunderstood the child's pleas for help. Instead of pulling them up from the cliff, it gave them a second thick unibrow on top of the first one.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine the sun noticing its mistake and apologizing to the child. "Ah, two thick unibrows wasn't what you wanted? Wow, I'm sorry. This is embarrassing… I'm actually not sure I can remove the second one now that it's on there."

kanji.meaningMnemonic: What you <radical>say</radical> is <radical>righteousness</radical>, at least that's what you believe. You keep talking and saying these things, and you don't listen to anyone else's words. This is your style of <kanji>deliberation</kanji> and <kanji>discussion</kanji>.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: In the <radical>animal</radical> kingdom, a <radical>snake</radical> is the most likely to commit a <kanji>crime</kanji>. Remember the Garden of Eden? The snake was the bad guy. Ever since then, snakes are the animal most known for committing crimes.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Tying a <radical>thread</radical> to a <radical>tombstone</radical> each year will help you <kanji>manage</kanji> the <kanji>passage of time</kanji>. That way you’ll know how many years you <kanji>pass through</kanji>, since you’ll have one thread for each year to count.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture yourself coming back to the same tombstone each passing year and tying a new thread to it. You're trying to manage these feelings by keeping track of how much time passes.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Dealing with the <kanji>passage of time</kanji> can be tough, so after you tie the thread to the tombstone, each year you eat a piece of <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい). You already eat cake to mark the passage of time on your birthday, so why not here as well?
kanji.readingHint: Maybe this is your future tombstone, and eating cake each year is a preemptive celebration of your death day! Ooh, what a spooky theory.

rawData.meaning_hint: Picture yourself coming back to the same tombstone each passing year and tying a new thread to it. You're trying to manage these feelings by keeping track of how much time passes.
rawData.reading_hint: Maybe this is your future tombstone, and eating cake each year is a preemptive celebration of your death day! Ooh, what a spooky theory.
rawData.meaning_mnemonic: Tying a <radical>thread</radical> to a <radical>tombstone</radical> each year will help you <kanji>manage</kanji> the <kanji>passage of time</kanji>. That way you’ll know how many years you <kanji>pass through</kanji>, since you’ll have one thread for each year to count.
rawData.reading_mnemonic: Dealing with the <kanji>passage of time</kanji> can be tough, so after you tie the thread to the tombstone, each year you eat a piece of <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい). You already eat cake to mark the passage of time on your birthday, so why not here as well?

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You walk into this <radical>tree</radical> and hit your <radical>head</radical> on it, then a <radical>knife</radical> falls out of it to <kanji>stab</kanji> you. Ow! What the heck?! What was a knife doing in that tree?
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine the pain and confusion of being stabbed by a violent tree. You hit your head on the tree again, as if to test it, and again a knife falls out and stabs you. Oh, so it's a stabbing tree, is it? That does it.
kanji.readingMnemonic: There's nothing more dangerous than a tree that likes to <kanji>stab</kanji>, so you get your <reading>saw</reading> (さ) and start to saw it down. Trees that stab get sawed.
kanji.readingHint: Feel the grain of the saw's wooden handle in your bare hand. As the saw bites into tree bark, a retaliatory rain of knives comes stabbing down around you. But you don't even flinch. You will saw this stabbing tree down.

rawData.reading_mnemonic: There's nothing more dangerous than a tree that likes to <kanji>stab</kanji>, so you get your <reading>saw</reading> (さ) and start to saw it down. Trees that stab get sawed.

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic:
When you have the <kanji>same</kanji> <kanji>feeling</kanji> as someone else, you are in <vocabulary>agreement</vocabulary> with them. They have an opinion or feeling about something, and you're <vocabulary>feeling the same</vocabulary> way.

同感 is a word used to express agreement, often with a sense of shared feeling. You use it when you hear a thought or viewpoint that really resonates with you — you totally agree with this person's take and feel exactly the same way. Preach!
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

同感 is a word used to express agreement, often with a sense of shared feeling. You use it when you hear a thought or viewpoint that really resonates with you — you totally agree with this person's take and feel exactly the same way. Preach!

context_sentences.en: I agree with that opinion as well.
context_sentences.ja: 私もその意見に同感ですね。

context_sentences.en: I agree with this more wholeheartedly than I've ever agreed with anything in my whole life.
context_sentences.ja: 今までにこんなに同感したことがないぐらい、思いっきり同感しています。

context_sentences.en: "I hate Fugu." "I feel the same way."
context_sentences.ja: 「私、フグのこと嫌い。」「同感だわ。」

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic: You know how 文句 is a <vocabulary>complaint</vocabulary>, right? Well, add する to make it a verb and you have <vocabulary>to complain</vocabulary>. This is a really casual way to complain though, usually used in manga or on social media. Keep your eye out for it!
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

context_sentences.en: I was just saying my opinion, but my boss yelled, "don’t complain," at me.
context_sentences.ja: 意見を言っただけなのに、上司に「文句するな」と怒鳴られました。
